text:
Admission Date:  2015-07-02              Discharge Date:   2016-07-31

Date of Birth:  2009-04-14             Sex:   M

Service: SURGERY

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:Seth
Chief Complaint:
s/p trauma, high speed car vs. tree

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
1984-05-01 Intramedullary rod fixation of left
peritrochanteric fracture with subtrochanteric extension.


History of Present Illness:
Patient is a 54 year old male who was transferred to us from an
OSH after a high speed car vs. a tree accident. Patient was
intubated at the OSH. Patient was intoxicated. He had multiple
known injuries including right retroperitoneal hematoma, SAH,
right rib fractures, later found to have right malleolal
fracture and T-spine compression fracature.

Past Medical History:
PMH: alcohol, tobacco, opiate abuse, HL, HTN. CRI after a
detox/rehab stay in CA
.
PSH: none


Social History:
Recently lost his job
History of ETOH abuse
Tobacco current

Family History:
non contributory

Physical Exam:
Gen: WD/WN, comfortable, slight distress when eyes open.
HEENT: small 1cm lac above left eye / right ear with abrasion vs
lac on pinna/ blood on left supra auricular area but no obvious
laceration noted.  No raccoons or battles sign.  Right TM
without
hemotympanum / left unable to visualize. Intubated / Pupils 09-30
bilaterally and brisk eomi / conjugate gaze.
Pupils: 09-30        EOMis
Neck:  in collar
Neuro:
Mental status: opens eyes to voice /lethargic, attempts to
cooperate with exam, normal affect.
Orientation: Oriented to O.
Recall: unable.
Language: intubated


Pertinent Results:
2015-07-02 09:15PM   WBC-27.9* RBC-4.12* HGB-12.8* HCT-38.4*
MCV-93 MCH-31.2 MCHC-33.4 RDW-15.2
2015-07-02 09:15PM   PLT COUNT-278
2015-07-02 09:15PM   PT-13.4 PTT-25.8 INR(PT)-1.1
2015-07-02 09:15PM   ASA-NEG ETHANOL-199* ACETMNPHN-NEG
bnzodzpn-NEG barbitrt-NEG tricyclic-NEG
2015-07-02 09:15PM   UREA N-16 CREAT-1.2

2015-07-02 Head CT : Bilateral, right greater than left,
subarachnoid hemorrhage. No herniation or significant mass
effect.

2015-07-02 CT Torso:

1. Large right retroperitoneal bleed with no definite evidence
of active
contrast extravasation.
2. Mesenteric hemorrhage/hematoma. A focally distended loop of
small bowel
with no wall thickening in the left lower quadrant is
nonspecific but can
represent early ischemic bowel.
3. Hematoma surrounding the second and third portion of the
duodenum with an apparent defect in the outer aspect of third
portion of the duodenum. While this can represent mass effect
from the adjacent hematoma bowel injury can not be excluded.
Close clinical follow-up and low threshold for repeat imaging is
recommended.
4. Perfusion defect within the right kidney compatible with
infarction, can be secondary to a thrombosed vessel.
5. Comminuted and displaced subtrochanteric left femur fracture
with a
fracture of the greater trochanter. Bilateral rib fractures,
third through
sixth right and third through seventh left anterior ribs and
10th through 12th posterior ribs. Compression deformity of T10
vertebral body is age
indeterminate, however morphology is compatible with chronicity.


2015-07-02 Left hip :

There is a comminuted intratrochanteric fracture with varus
angulation and 60% shaft with medial displacement of the distal
fragment. Mild degenerative changes of the left hip are
suggested. Lateral view is difficult to accurately interpret,
but confirms the presence of an intratrochanteric fracture. The
lateral view suggests some anterior offset of the distal
fragment, but minimal angulation. The femoral acetabular
relationship is difficult to confirm on the lateral view, but
this is best assessed on a CT scan from 2015-07-02 at 21:33 p.m.,
which showed anatomic femoroacetabular alignment.

1991-04-13 CTA Head: 1. CT head demonstrates subarachnoid
hemorrhage not significantly changed since 2015-07-02.
2. CT angiography of the head demonstrates no evidence of an
aneurysm greater than 3 mm in size.

1984-05-01 Right ankle : Fracturs of the medial malleolus and
--likely --lateral talus.





Brief Hospital Course:
Patient was admitted to the TSICU on acute care surgery from the
trauma bay.

Neuro: Patient was intubated on admission. Two sequential CT
scans showed stable SAH, and neurosurgery deemed it
non-operative. He received phenytoin for seizure prophylaxis. He
showed transient signs of agitation c/w alcohol withdrawal and
these were controlled with diazepam per CIWA scale.

MSK : He was taken to the Operating Room on 1984-05-01 for ORIF of
his left subtrochanteric fracture.  He tolerated the procedure
well and returned to the ICU in stable condition and remained
intubated.  His pain was well controlled.  He had some
ecchymosis noted around his right ankle and subsequent Xrays
revealed a right medial malleolus fracture which would simply be
treated with an air cast boot.

Pulm: Patient was intubated at the OSH. He continued to be
intubated secondary to sedation and agitation. He failed
extubation on HD 4. He had BAL on 01-10 which showed 1+ PMNs and
no orgs. The failure to extubate was attributed to possible
episode of aspiration prior to intubation. Patient was
subsequently given one dose of vancomycin and Zosyn.
Re-assessment found alcohol withdrawal to be the most likely
aetiology and antibiotics were stopped with resolution of fever.
On HD #6, the patient was extubated and tolerated room air
thereafter.

GI/FEN: Patient was NPO and had serial abdominal exams due to
his retroperitoneal hematoma.  There was no evidence of active
bleeding and his abdomen was soft. Once extubated, he passed
speech and swallow evaluation and tolerated regular diet
thereafter.

GU: No issues.

Heme: Patient sustained a R retroperitoneal hematoma which was
monitored by serial HCT. CT scan did not show active
extravasation of contrast. Once there was no further evidence of
hemorrhage, the patient was started on DVT prophylaxis.

ID: Patient received one dose of vancomycin and Zosyn for
presumed aspiration pneumonia which was then discounted in favor
of alcohol withdrawal.

Following transfer to the Surgical floor he was evaluated by the
Physical Therapy service and they worked with him frequently to
help him mobilize as his weight bearing status was " as
tolerated" bilaterally.  With time and effort he was able to get
out of bed to chair and take a few steps.  His neurologic status
was stable and the Neurosurgery service would like to check
another Head CT in 4 weeks.  He had no deficits.  He was eating
well and his pain was well controlled with MS Contin.  His
surgical wound was healing well.  He was Lovenox for DVT
prophylaxis and was able to administer it to himself without
difficulty.  He was discharged home on 2016-07-31 and will follow
up in the San Diego County Psychiatric Hospital Clinic in 2 weeks.  See discharge
information for other appointments.


Medications on Admission:
Citalopram 40 mg daily
Lisinopril 10 mg daily

Discharge Medications:
1. nicotine 21 mg/24 hr Patch 24 hr Sig: One (1) Patch 24 hr
Transdermal DAILY (Daily).

Disp:*28 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2*

Subcutaneous Q12H (every 12 hours).
Disp:*28 mg* Refills:*2*

times a day).
5. clonidine 0.3 mg/24 hr Patch Weekly Sig: 0.3 Patch Weekly
Transdermal QWED (every Wednesday).
Disp:*2 Patch Weekly(s)* Refills:*2*

Disp:*14 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2*

Sustained Release PO Q12H (every 12 hours).
Disp:*28 Tablet Sustained Release(s)* Refills:*0*

(Every 8 Hours).
9. senna 8.6 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO HS (at bedtime).
10. tramadol 50 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO QID (4 times a
day).
Disp:*56 Tablet(s)* Refills:*2*
11. magnesium hydroxide 400 mg/5 mL Suspension Sig: Thirty (30)
ML PO at bedtime as needed for constipation.
12. morphine 15 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO every four (4)
hours as needed for pain.
Disp:*84 Tablet(s)* Refills:*0*


Discharge Disposition:
Home

Discharge Diagnosis:
S/P car v. tree
1. Right retroperitoneal hematoma
2. Subarachnoid hemorrhage
3. Left intertrochanteric hip fracture with subtrochanteric
extension
4. Right anterior rib fractures 10-05
5. Left anterior rib fractures 12-12 and posterior 05-05
6. Compression fracture T spine
7. Right medial malleolar fracture
8. Acute blood loss anemia

Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - requires assistance or aid (walker
or cane).


Discharge Instructions:
* You were admitted to the hospital with multiple injuries
following your accident including broken bones and internal
injuries.
*  Your injury caused multiple bilateral rib fractures which can
cause severe pain and subsequently cause you to take shallow
breaths because of the pain.

*  You should take your pain medication as directed to stay
ahead of the pain otherwise you won't be able to take deep
breaths. If the pain medication is too sedating take half the
dose and notify your physician.

*  Pneumonia is a complication of rib fractures.  In order to
decrease your risk you must use your incentive spirometer 4
times every hour while awake.  This will help expand the small
airways in your lungs and assist in coughing up secretions that
pool in the lungs.

*  You will be more comfortable if you use a cough pillow to
hold against your chest and guard your rib cage while coughing
and deep breathing.

*  Symptomatic relief with ice packs or heating pads for short
periods may ease the pain.

*  Narcotic pain medication can cause constipation therefore you
should take a stool softener twice daily and increase your fluid
and fiber intake if possible.

*  Do NOT smoke

*  Return to the Emergency Room right away for any acute
shortness of breath, increased pain or crackling sensation
around your ribs ( crepitus ).

* Continue to take your Lovenox twice daily as directed by the
Orthopedic service.

* Your weight bearing status is weight bearing as tolerated for
both lower extremities as reviewed by the Physical Therapist.

* Wear your air cast boot on your right leg.

* The free care pharmacy has given you 2 weeks of free
prescriptions but anything over the counter like Tylenol or
stool softeners will need to be paid out of pocket.

* If you have any new symptoms that concern you please call the
Acute Care Clinic.

Followup Instructions:
Call the San Diego County Psychiatric Hospital Clinic at 740-698-9576 for a follow up
appointment in 2 weeks

Call the Acute Care Clinic at 928-968-7677 for a follow up
appointment in 10-10 weeks.

Call the University Medical Center Of Southern Nevada Clinic at 765-711-8311 for a follow up
appointment in 4 weeks with Dr. Butler with a repeat head
CT.



Completed by:2016-07-31
